disease.name: Budd-Chiari Syndrome
disease.note: Hepatic venous outflow obstruction at any level from the small hepatic veins to the junction of the inferior vena cava with the right atrium. Primary (thrombotic) is most common, strongly associated with underlying thrombophilia. Secondary (extrinsic compression) occurs from tumor, abscess, cyst. Untreated leads to hepatic congestion, fibrosis, cirrhosis, and liver failure.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Fulminant/acute -> EMERGENCY; Subacute with ascites -> URGENT; Chronic compensated -> URGENT workup

historyFeatures.note: PV, ET, factor V Leiden, prothrombin mutation, antiphospholipid syndrome, PNH

examFeatures.note: On flanks and back (not just caput medusae); blood flows upward from groin
Leg swelling from DVT, livedo reticularis from APLS

bedside.name: JVP assessment and hepatojugular reflux
bedside.indication: rule out right heart failure / constrictive pericarditis

laboratory.name: LFTs (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT, bilirubin)
laboratory.indication: elevated; ALT/AST may be markedly elevated in acute; ALP/GGT often elevated in chronic

laboratory.name: FBC with differential
laboratory.indication: elevated Hb/Hct suggests PV; elevated platelets suggest ET

laboratory.name: Thrombophilia screen
laboratory.indication: factor V Leiden, prothrombin G20210A, antiphospholipid antibodies (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, beta2GP), protein C/S/antithrombin (interpret with caution in acute phase/blood products)

laboratory.name: JAK2 V617F mutation screen
laboratory.indication: screen for occult MPN; positive in 40-50% of BCS

laboratory.name: PNH flow cytometry
laboratory.indication: paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria screen if other causes negative

laboratory.name: Ascitic fluid analysis
laboratory.indication: high protein (>2.5g/dL) and low SAAG (<1.1) is classic for BCS (hepatic outflow obstruction causing high protein ascites)

laboratory.name: AFP and CA 19-9
laboratory.indication: screening for HCC/cholangiocarcinoma if chronic BCS

laboratory.name: Viral hepatitis serology
laboratory.indication: rule out other causes of liver disease

imaging.name: Doppler ultrasound abdomen
imaging.indication: first-line: absent or reversed hepatic vein flow, intrahepatic collaterals (spider-web pattern), thrombus in hepatic vein/IVC, caudate lobe hypertrophy, portal vein patency

imaging.name: CT abdomen with IV contrast (triphasic)
imaging.indication: hepatic vein non-opacification, caudate hypertrophy, patchy liver enhancement ('nutmeg' pattern), ascites, collaterals

imaging.name: MRI abdomen with MR venography
imaging.indication: detailed hepatic vein and IVC anatomy, thrombus characterization, flow quantification; may replace invasive venography

imaging.name: Hepatic venography and IVC venography
imaging.indication: invasive but definitive: spider-web pattern of collaterals; pressure gradient measurement; for pre-TIPS assessment

imaging.name: Liver biopsy
imaging.indication: not always needed if imaging diagnostic. Shows: centrilobular congestion, hepatocyte necrosis, zone 3 fibrosis (early); cardiac cirrhosis-like pattern (late). Transjugular route preferred due to coagulopathy/ascites.

management.protocolReference: AASLD Practice Guidelines: Budd-Chiari Syndrome; EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines: Vascular Liver Diseases

pathways.criteria: Recent onset symptoms, no cirrhosis, no decompensation, treatable underlying thrombophilia
pathways.treatment: Anticoagulation: LMWH (enoxaparin 1mg/kg SC BID or weight-based full dose) followed by warfarin (target INR 2-3) or DOAC (rivaroxaban 15-20mg PO daily; apixaban 5mg PO BID). Duration: lifelong (given underlying thrombophilia). Treat underlying cause: MPN cytoreduction (hydroxyurea), JAK2 inhibitors (ruxolitinib), phlebotomy for PV. Manage ascites: diuretics, sodium restriction, TIPs if refractory. Monitor for response with clinical improvement, LFTs, and repeat imaging in 1-3 months.

pathways.criteria: Persistent ascites despite anticoagulation and diuretics, progressive LFT deterioration
pathways.treatment: TIPS (Transjugular Intrahepatic Portosystemic Shunt). Indicated for patients who fail medical therapy with anticoagulation. TIPS decompresses the liver by creating a shunt between portal vein and hepatic vein. Technical success >90%. Complications: shunt stenosis/dysfunction (re-intervention 30-50%), encephalopathy (20-30%). Covered stents preferred. Post-TIPS: continue anticoagulation lifelong.

pathways.criteria: Rapid onset liver failure (INR >2, encephalopathy, bilirubin >5mg/dL) within 4-8 weeks
pathways.treatment: Urgent transplant evaluation and listing. This is rare but carries high mortality without transplant. TIPS may be attempted as bridge (limited evidence in fulminant). Anticoagulation continues. No role for TIPS if no suitable hepatic vein remnant. Liver transplant: excellent 5-year survival (80-90%). Recurrence in graft possible if underlying thrombophilia not managed (lifelong anticoagulation post-transplant).

pathways.criteria: Long-standing BCS with established cirrhosis, portal hypertension, possible varices
pathways.treatment: Anticoagulation (lifelong). Variceal primary prophylaxis (beta-blockers or EVL). HCC surveillance US + AFP q6m (chronic BCS carries HCC risk). Manage complications. If decompensation develops: TIPS or liver transplant evaluation. Monitor for progression with regular imaging and LFTs.

pathways.criteria: Recurrent ascites or variceal bleeding after TIPS; Doppler suggests shunt stenosis
pathways.treatment: Doppler ultrasound for screening (peak velocity <90 or >190cm/s or change in velocity). Portal venography + TIPS revision: balloon angioplasty + restenting. Covered stents reduce dysfunction vs bare stents. Continue anticoagulation.

disease.emergencyPriority: high for acute/fulminant; medium for chronic
